---
title: Homebrew
subtitle: Installing Homebrew on a non-admin macOS account
---

This documents the proper way to install Homebrew for a non-admin
account on a Mac.

Homebrew expects to install at `/opt/homebrew` (Apple Silicon) or
`/usr/local` (Intel). The parent directory `/opt` is owned by `root`
with `755` permissions, so:

- A non-admin account cannot create `/opt/homebrew` itself.
- Homebrew's official install script also refuses to run at all for a
  non-admin user, even though once the target directory exists and is
  owned by you, no further admin access is actually needed.

The fix is a **one-time bridge**: use the admin account to create and
hand over an empty, correctly-owned directory, then install entirely
as your normal user from that point on.

## Steps (run from your non-admin account's terminal)

**1. Confirm architecture and check `/opt` ownership**
```zsh
uname -m           # arm64 = Apple Silicon, x86_64 = Intel
ls -ld /opt        # confirm it's root-owned, as expected
```

The first command will return `arm64` on an Apple Silicon machine or `x86_64` on an Intel machine. The second command will confirm that the directory `/opt` is root-owned, as expected.

**2. Use the admin account to create and hand over the `/opt` directory**

Replace `username` with the actual non-admin username:
```zsh
su admin -c "mkdir -p /opt/homebrew && chown username:staff /opt/homebrew"
```

When prompted for a password, enter the *admin account* password.

**3. Verify ownership before proceeding**
```zsh
ls -ld /opt/homebrew
```
Should show your non-admin username as owner.

**4a. Try the normal installer as non-admin**
```zsh
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
```

If `/opt/homebrew` was empty and correctly owned by you, this
typically still requires sudo internally and may print:
```
Need sudo access on macOS (e.g. the user username needs to be an Administrator)!
```
The installer checks for admin rights unconditionally, regardless of
whether it's actually needed for your specific case. If you hit this:

**4b. Run the installer as admin instead, then transfer ownership**
```zsh
su admin -c '/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"'
su admin -c "sudo chown -R username:staff /opt/homebrew"
```
Replace `username` with the non-admin username. When prompted for a password, enter the admin password.

**5. Verify**
```zsh
ls -ld /opt/homebrew
which brew
brew --version
```

From this point forward, **no further admin involvement is needed** —
`brew install`, `brew update`, etc. all work normally as a regular
user, since every file under `/opt/homebrew` is now owned by that account.

## PATH setup (in `.zprofile`)

Homebrew needs to be added to the PATH variable in order to be used, but it needs to supersede system-provided tools of the same name (*e.g.*, `openssl`). This is accomplished by *prepending* it to the existing PATH variable rather appending. Add the following line to `.zprofile`:
```{.zsh filename=".zprofile"}
# Homebrew
path=("/opt/homebrew/bin" $path)
```

Run `brew doctor` after this to confirm no path-ordering warnings.

## Keg-only formulae (*e.g.*, Python)

Homebrew's Python formulae are keg-only by design. Installing
`python@3.13` does not create a plain `python`/`pip` command.
Two bad options to avoid:

- `brew link --force python@3.13`: This works, but if you ever have two 
  Python formulae installed (e.g. `python@3.11` and `python@3.13`), whichever was force-linked most recently silently wins, and the unversioned name can flip without warning on a future `brew install`.
- Plain aliases or shell functions for `python`/`pip`: These work for
  running commands, but `which`/`whence python` then reports the
  function/alias definition instead of a real file path, which breaks
  the common habit of using `which` to verify Python environment activation.

**Best option: a personal `~/bin` directory with real symlinks.**
```zsh
mkdir -p ~/bin
ln -s /opt/homebrew/bin/python3.13 ~/bin/python
ln -s /opt/homebrew/bin/pip3.13 ~/bin/pip
```

Add `~/bin` to PATH in `.zprofile`, **after** the Homebrew line (lower
priority — it should only fill in names Homebrew doesn't already
provide):
```{.zsh filename=".zprofile"}
# Homebrew
path+=("$HOME/bin")
path=("/opt/homebrew/bin" $path)
```

This keeps `which python` / `which pip` reporting a real file path,
both outside and inside a virtual environment (venv). Activating a venv
prepends `.venv/bin` ahead of everything else on PATH automatically,
so no extra logic is needed to give virtual environments priority.

## Reinstalling formulae and rebuilding venvs

Once Homebrew itself is set up, install any needed:
```zsh
brew install <formula1> <formula2> ...
```
For example:

- `gh`: GitHub CLI for using GitHub in terminals
- `python@3.x`, where `x` is a specific version of Python

Any existing Python virtual environments must be rebuilt from
scratch — they cannot simply be redirected at a new Python install,
since each venv's interpreter, paths, and shebangs are tied to the
exact binary that created it. To find all existing virtual environments on the machine created using Homebrew:
```zsh
find ~ \( -path "*/node_modules" -o -path "*/Library" -o -path "*/.cache" \) -prune -o -name "pyvenv.cfg" -print 2>/dev/null
```

or:
```zsh
find ~ -name "pyvenv.cfg" 2>/dev/null -exec grep -l "homebrew" {} \;
```

Recreate each project virtual environment one at a time:

```zsh
cd /path/to/project        # Replace with one project directory at a time
rm -rf .venv               # Replace with the environment name, if not .venv
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip
pip install -r requirements.txt
deactivate
```

## Final check

Confirm everything worked:
```zsh
brew doctor
```
Should report no warnings. If it suggests adding a PATH line directly
to `.zshrc`, ignore that specific suggestion if you're already
managing PATH cleanly in `.zprofile` to avoid ending up with two
competing PATH definitions.
